gate_type: capability check manage_options (global options) + wp_nonce admin-only + capability check unfiltered_html (per-post meta)
reasoning: Two write paths exist, neither reachable by unauthenticated or default low-privilege attackers. (1) Global options path: register_setting() with 'trim' sanitization is protected by WordPress Settings API which enforces manage_options capability — strictly admin-only on both single-site and multisite. (2) Per-post meta path: post_meta_save() requires a valid wp_nonce verified against 'jamify_hfs_post_meta_save', which is only generated by meta_setup() when rendering the post edit meta box in the admin area (line 286). The nonce is therefore only obtainable by logged-in users who have access to the post editor. Additionally, even after nonce verification, a second gate at line 320 checks current_user_can('unfiltered_html') before persisting the script content — on single-site this capability belongs only to admins by default; on multisite it is restricted to super-admins regardless of plugin settings. The optional privilege escalation (update_role_author/update_role_contributor granting unfiltered_html via add_cap) is itself gated behind an admin deliberately enabling 'jamify_hfs_allow_author' or 'jamify_hfs_allow_contributor' options. An unauthenticated attacker cannot obtain the nonce (generated only in authenticated admin page render) and cannot satisfy the capability checks. Even a default Author or Contributor on a standard install lacks unfiltered_html unless an admin explicitly grants it via the plugin settings. The sinks are confirmed real and intentionally unescaped, but neither write path is reachable without admin action or an authenticated session with explicitly-granted unfiltered_html capability. The do_shortcode() execution in wp_footer is a legitimate additional risk surface for admin-controlled content but does not change exploitability by external attackers. Severity is downgraded from MEDIUM to LOW because the primary risk (admin storing scripts) is by-design WordPress behavior (admins are trusted), and the escalation vector requires explicit admin opt-in — making this an administrative misconfiguration risk rather than a default-exploitable vulnerability.
